questions.stem: A constant horizontal force of $20\ \text{N}$ drags a box $5\ \text{m}$ along a horizontal floor in the direction of the force. How much work does the force do?
questions.explanation: For a force parallel to the displacement, the work is simply $W = Fd = 20 \times 5 = 100\ \text{J}$. The trap is $4\ \text{J}$, which divides the force by the distance instead of multiplying.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A force of $20\ \text{N}$ pulls a sled $5\ \text{m}$ along level ground, but the rope makes an angle of $60^\circ$ above the horizontal. How much work does the force do?
questions.explanation: Only the component of force along the displacement does work: $W = Fd\cos\theta = 20 \times 5 \times \cos 60^\circ = 100 \times 0.5 = 50\ \text{J}$. The trap is $100\ \text{J}$, which forgets the cosine and uses the full force as if it were horizontal.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A waiter carries a tray of weight $15\ \text{N}$ horizontally across a room, holding it at constant height. How much work does he do on the tray?
options: A negative amount equal to the weight times the distance
questions.explanation: The force the waiter exerts is vertical (holding the tray up), while the displacement is horizontal, so the force and displacement are perpendicular and $W = Fd\cos 90^\circ = 0$. No work is done on the tray however far he walks. The trap is the first option, which multiplies the weight by the horizontal distance as if the upward force did work along a horizontal path.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $4\ \text{kg}$ object moves at $5\ \text{m/s}$. What is its kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(4)(5^2) = \tfrac{1}{2}(4)(25) = 50\ \text{J}$. The trap is $100\ \text{J}$, which omits the factor of one half and computes $mv^2$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A net force does $36\ \text{J}$ of work on a $2\ \text{kg}$ object initially at rest on a frictionless surface. What is the object's final speed?
questions.explanation: By the work-energy theorem the net work equals the change in kinetic energy: $W = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 - 0$. So $36 = \tfrac{1}{2}(2)v^2 = v^2$, giving $v = 6\ \text{m/s}$. The trap is $18\ \text{m/s}$, which sets $W = mv$ and solves $36 = 2v$, treating work as if it were momentum.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ ball is lifted at constant speed to a height of $10\ \text{m}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the gain in its gravitational potential energy?
questions.explanation: The change in gravitational potential energy is $\Delta U = mgh = 2 \times 10 \times 10 = 200\ \text{J}$. The trap is $20\ \text{J}$, which uses $mh$ and leaves out the factor of $g$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A ball is dropped from rest from a height of $20\ \text{m}$. Using energy conservation with $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and no air resistance, what is its speed just before it hits the ground?
questions.explanation: All the potential energy converts to kinetic energy: $mgh = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$, so $v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 20} = \sqrt{400} = 20\ \text{m/s}$. The mass cancels. The trap is $400\ \text{m/s}$, which reports $v^2 = 400$ as the speed, forgetting to take the square root.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A pendulum bob is released from rest at a height of $0.45\ \text{m}$ above the lowest point of its swing. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore friction. What is its speed at the lowest point?
questions.explanation: Energy conservation along the arc gives $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = mgh$, so $v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 0.45} = \sqrt{9} = 3\ \text{m/s}$. The path is curved, but only the change in height matters because the string tension does no work. The trap is $9\ \text{m/s}$, which reports $v^2 = 9$ as the speed.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A motor does $600\ \text{J}$ of work in $4\ \text{s}$. What is its average power output?
questions.explanation: Power is work per unit time: $P = W/t = 600/4 = 150\ \text{W}$. The trap is $2400\ \text{W}$, which multiplies work by time instead of dividing.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A car engine exerts a constant driving force of $200\ \text{N}$ while the car moves at a steady $15\ \text{m/s}$. What power does the engine deliver?
questions.explanation: For a force along the direction of motion, power is force times speed: $P = Fv = 200 \times 15 = 3000\ \text{W}$. The trap is $13.3\ \text{W}$, which divides force by speed instead of multiplying.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A spring with stiffness constant $k = 200\ \text{N/m}$ is compressed by $0.1\ \text{m}$ from its natural length. How much elastic potential energy is stored?
questions.explanation: The energy stored in a spring is $U = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(200)(0.1)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(200)(0.01) = 1\ \text{J}$. The trap is $20\ \text{J}$, which uses $kx$ and so leaves out both the factor of one half and the squaring of the displacement, giving the spring force times displacement without the averaging.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A block of mass $0.5\ \text{kg}$ is pushed against a spring of stiffness $k = 200\ \text{N/m}$, compressing it $0.2\ \text{m}$, then released on a frictionless surface. What speed does the block reach as it leaves the spring?
questions.explanation: The spring energy converts entirely to kinetic energy: $\tfrac{1}{2}kx^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$. The stored energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}(200)(0.04) = 4\ \text{J}$, so $\tfrac{1}{2}(0.5)v^2 = 4$, giving $v^2 = 16$ and $v = 4\ \text{m/s}$. The trap is $2\ \text{m/s}$, which sets $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ equal to $kx^2$ without the one half on the spring side, halving the available energy.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $1000\ \text{kg}$ car speeds up from $10\ \text{m/s}$ to $20\ \text{m/s}$. How much work does the net force do on it?
questions.explanation: By the work-energy theorem, $W = \tfrac{1}{2}m(v_f^2 - v_i^2) = \tfrac{1}{2}(1000)(400 - 100) = 500 \times 300 = 150{,}000\ \text{J}$. The trap is $50{,}000\ \text{J}$, which uses $\tfrac{1}{2}m(v_f - v_i)^2 = 500(10)^2$, squaring the difference of speeds rather than taking the difference of the squares.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ block is released from rest and slides $5\ \text{m}$ down a plane inclined at $37^\circ$ ($\sin 37^\circ = 0.6$, $\cos 37^\circ = 0.8$). The coefficient of kinetic friction is $0.5$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. Using energy methods, what is its kinetic energy at the bottom of the $5\ \text{m}$?
questions.explanation: The block descends a vertical height $h = 5\sin 37^\circ = 3\ \text{m}$, releasing $mgh = 2(10)(3) = 60\ \text{J}$ of potential energy. Friction does negative work $f_k d = \mu_k mg\cos\theta \cdot d = 0.5(2)(10)(0.8)(5) = 40\ \text{J}$. The kinetic energy gained is $60 - 40 = 20\ \text{J}$. The trap is $60\ \text{J}$, which counts the full potential energy drop and forgets that friction removed part of it.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Which of the following is a conservative force, for which the work done around any closed path is zero?
options: The push of a hand that varies with position in a non-recoverable way
questions.explanation: Gravity is conservative: the work it does depends only on the change in height, not the path, so around a closed loop the net work is zero and a potential energy can be defined. Kinetic friction and air resistance are dissipative; they always oppose motion and remove energy, so the work around a closed path is negative, not zero. The trap is kinetic friction, which is the classic example of a non-conservative force.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A person of mass $60\ \text{kg}$ climbs a flight of stairs of vertical height $3\ \text{m}$ in $5\ \text{s}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the average power they develop against gravity?
questions.explanation: The work against gravity is $mgh = 60(10)(3) = 1800\ \text{J}$, done in $5\ \text{s}$, so the power is $P = 1800/5 = 360\ \text{W}$. The trap is $1800\ \text{W}$, which reports the work as if it were the power and skips the division by time.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A pump raises $600\ \text{kg}$ of water through a height of $10\ \text{m}$ in $20\ \text{s}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the minimum power needed?
questions.explanation: The work to lift the water is $mgh = 600(10)(10) = 60{,}000\ \text{J}$, delivered in $20\ \text{s}$, so $P = 60{,}000/20 = 3000\ \text{W}$. The trap is $60{,}000\ \text{W}$, which quotes the total work as the power and omits the time.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $3\ \text{kg}$ object has linear momentum of magnitude $12\ \text{kg}\,\text{m/s}$. What is its kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy and momentum are linked by $KE = p^2/(2m) = 12^2/(2 \times 3) = 144/6 = 24\ \text{J}$. Equivalently, $v = p/m = 4\ \text{m/s}$ and $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}(3)(16) = 24\ \text{J}$. The trap is $72\ \text{J}$, which computes $\tfrac{1}{2}pv$ wrongly or uses $p^2/m$ without the factor of $2$ in the denominator.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A roller-coaster car of mass $500\ \text{kg}$ starts from rest at the top of a frictionless hill $40\ \text{m}$ high. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is its speed at a point $15\ \text{m}$ above the ground?
questions.explanation: Only the drop in height matters. The car descends $40 - 15 = 25\ \text{m}$, so $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = mg(25)$ and $v^2 = 2(10)(25) = 500$, giving $v = \sqrt{500} \approx 22.4\ \text{m/s}$. The mass cancels. The trap is $\sqrt{800} \approx 28.3\ \text{m/s}$, which uses the full $40\ \text{m}$ height and forgets that the car is still $15\ \text{m}$ up.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A force $\mathbf{F} = (3\,\hat{\imath} + 4\,\hat{\jmath})\ \text{N}$ acts on an object that undergoes a displacement $\mathbf{d} = (2\,\hat{\imath} + 1\,\hat{\jmath})\ \text{m}$. How much work does the force do?
questions.explanation: Work is the dot product of force and displacement: $W = \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{d} = (3)(2) + (4)(1) = 6 + 4 = 10\ \text{J}$. The trap is $25\ \text{J}$, which multiplies the magnitudes $|\mathbf{F}| = 5\ \text{N}$ and $|\mathbf{d}| = \sqrt{5}\ \text{m}$ ignoring the angle, or otherwise treats the vectors as parallel.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ object moving at $10\ \text{m/s}$ is brought to rest by a constant friction force over a distance of $25\ \text{m}$. What is the magnitude of the friction force?
questions.explanation: By the work-energy theorem the friction work equals the kinetic energy removed: $fd = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$, so $f(25) = \tfrac{1}{2}(2)(100) = 100\ \text{J}$ and $f = 100/25 = 4\ \text{N}$. The trap is $8\ \text{N}$, which forgets the factor of one half in the kinetic energy and uses $mv^2/d = 200/25$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Two objects, one of mass $m$ and one of mass $2m$, have the same kinetic energy. What is the ratio of their speeds $v_m : v_{2m}$?
questions.explanation: Equal kinetic energy means $\tfrac{1}{2}m v_m^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(2m)v_{2m}^2$, so $v_m^2 = 2v_{2m}^2$ and $v_m/v_{2m} = \sqrt{2}$. The lighter object must move faster to carry the same energy, by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$. The trap is $1 : \sqrt{2}$, which inverts the ratio and makes the heavier object the faster one.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A block slides along a horizontal surface and is brought to rest by friction. From the point of view of mechanical energy, what happens to the block's kinetic energy?
options: It is converted to thermal energy by friction and is not recoverable as mechanical energy.
It is stored as gravitational potential energy.
It is destroyed, violating conservation of energy.
It is converted to elastic potential energy in the surface.
questions.explanation: Friction is non-conservative: it converts the ordered kinetic energy into disordered thermal energy (heat in the block and surface). Total energy is conserved, but the mechanical energy is not, because the heat cannot be fully recovered as motion. The trap is the claim that the energy is destroyed; energy is always conserved overall, it has merely changed form.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A ball of mass $0.2\ \text{kg}$ is thrown straight up and rises to a maximum height of $5\ \text{m}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. Using energy conservation, what was its launch speed?
questions.explanation: At the top all the kinetic energy has become potential energy: $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = mgh$, so $v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2(10)(5)} = \sqrt{100} = 10\ \text{m/s}$. The mass is irrelevant. The trap is $100\ \text{m/s}$, which quotes $v^2 = 100$ as the speed.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A constant force accelerates a $5\ \text{kg}$ trolley from rest. After $4\ \text{s}$ the trolley has travelled $16\ \text{m}$ on a frictionless track. What average power was delivered over this interval?
questions.explanation: First find the acceleration from $s = \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$: $16 = \tfrac{1}{2}a(16)$, so $a = 2\ \text{m/s}^2$ and the force is $F = ma = 10\ \text{N}$. The work done is $Fs = 10 \times 16 = 160\ \text{J}$, and the average power is $160/4 = 40\ \text{W}$. The trap is $160\ \text{W}$, which reports the total work as the power and forgets to divide by the $4\ \text{s}$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $4\ \text{kg}$ object moves at $5\ \text{m/s}$. A net force then does $30\ \text{J}$ of work on it. Find the original kinetic energy and the new speed.
questions.solution: The original kinetic energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 25 = $ {{1}} joules. By the work-energy theorem the new kinetic energy is $50 + 30 = 80\ \text{J}$, so $\tfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times v^2 = 80$ gives a new speed of $v = $ {{2}} metres per second.
questions.explanation: The initial kinetic energy is $50\ \text{J}$. Adding the $30\ \text{J}$ of work gives $80\ \text{J}$, so $v^2 = 80/2 = 40$ and $v = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32\ \text{m/s}$. The work-energy theorem adds work to kinetic energy, not directly to the speed.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A ball is dropped from rest from a height of $20\ \text{m}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. Using energy conservation, find its speed at the ground and the fraction of the way down at which half its potential energy has become kinetic.
questions.solution: Energy conservation gives $v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{400} = $ {{1}} metres per second. Half the potential energy has converted when the ball has fallen half the height, that is when it has descended {{2}} metres.
questions.explanation: The impact speed is $\sqrt{400} = 20\ \text{m/s}$. Since potential energy is $mgy$ measured from the ground, it falls to half its starting value when the height has dropped to half, after a descent of $10\ \text{m}$. The kinetic energy gained scales with the distance fallen.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A spring of stiffness $k = 200\ \text{N/m}$ is compressed $0.2\ \text{m}$ and then released to push a $0.5\ \text{kg}$ block along a frictionless surface. Find the stored energy and the launch speed of the block.
questions.solution: The stored elastic energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}kx^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 0.04 = $ {{1}} joules. Setting this equal to $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ gives $v = $ {{2}} metres per second. The quantity conserved as the spring releases is the total mechanical {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The stored energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}(200)(0.04) = 4\ \text{J}$. Then $\tfrac{1}{2}(0.5)v^2 = 4$ gives $v^2 = 16$ and $v = 4\ \text{m/s}$. On a frictionless surface the spring energy fully becomes kinetic energy, conserving mechanical energy.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A pump lifts $600\ \text{kg}$ of water through $10\ \text{m}$ in $20\ \text{s}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. Find the work done against gravity and the power required.
questions.solution: The work against gravity is $mgh = 600 \times 10 \times 10 = $ {{1}} joules. The power is this work divided by the time, $P = $ {{2}} watts.
questions.explanation: The lifting work is $60{,}000\ \text{J}$, and dividing by $20\ \text{s}$ gives $3000\ \text{W}$. Power is the rate of doing work, so the time always enters the denominator.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A force of $20\ \text{N}$ pulls a sled $5\ \text{m}$ along level ground, with the rope at $60^\circ$ above the horizontal. Find the work done, and state how it compares with the $100\ \text{J}$ that the same force would do if it pulled horizontally.
questions.solution: Only the horizontal component does work, so $W = Fd\cos 60^\circ = 20 \times 5 \times 0.5 = $ {{1}} joules. Compared with the horizontal case, this is {{2}} of the $100\ \text{J}$.
questions.explanation: With $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$ the work is $20(5)(0.5) = 50\ \text{J}$, exactly half the $100\ \text{J}$ a horizontal pull would do. The tilt of the rope reduces the effective force along the motion.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ block is released from rest and slides $5\ \text{m}$ down a plane inclined at $37^\circ$ ($\sin 37^\circ = 0.6$, $\cos 37^\circ = 0.8$) with coefficient of kinetic friction $0.5$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. Find the potential energy released, the friction work, and the kinetic energy at the bottom.
questions.solution: The vertical drop is $5 \times 0.6 = 3\ \text{m}$, so the potential energy released is $mgh = 2 \times 10 \times 3 = $ {{1}} joules. Friction removes $\mu_k mg\cos\theta \cdot d = 0.5 \times 2 \times 10 \times 0.8 \times 5 = $ {{2}} joules. The kinetic energy at the bottom is the difference, {{3}} joules.
questions.explanation: The released potential energy is $60\ \text{J}$, friction removes $40\ \text{J}$, so the kinetic energy gained is $60 - 40 = 20\ \text{J}$. The friction term uses the cosine of the incline angle, the height uses the sine.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics
